edges.description: Reduced TNAP activity is measured clinically as persistently low alkaline phosphatase activity.
edges.source: ALPL loss of function and TNAP deficiency

edges.description: Reduced TNAP activity produces the hallmark decreased circulating alkaline phosphatase phenotype.
edges.source: ALPL loss of function and TNAP deficiency

edges.description: Loss of TNAP enzyme activity leaves natural substrates extracellularly elevated.
edges.source: ALPL loss of function and TNAP deficiency

edges.description: PPi accumulates when TNAP pyrophosphatase activity is deficient.
PLP accumulates extracellularly when TNAP phosphomonoesterase activity is deficient.
PEA is another elevated ALP substrate used diagnostically.
PPi is a potent inhibitor of hydroxyapatite growth in bone and dental tissues.
PLP accumulation marks abnormal vitamin B6 metabolism that contributes to seizure vulnerability.
PPi excess can produce chondrocalcinosis and arthropathy.
Chronic HPP can include fatigue as part of the broader non-skeletal adult disease burden; the biochemical-to-fatigue intermediate remains uncertain.
Failed skeletal mineralization manifests as rickets in children and osteomalacia in adults.
Dentoalveolar mineralization defects produce premature loss of primary teeth.
Osteomalacia and impaired mineralization increase fracture and pseudofracture risk.
Adult and severe HPP musculoskeletal disease includes muscle weakness, though the exact intermediate between mineralization failure and weakness is not fully resolved.
Craniosynostosis is a severe skeletal manifestation of HPP, though the exact local mechanism is not fully resolved.
Severe skeletal hypomineralization with rib and thoracic involvement contributes to respiratory compromise in perinatal and infantile HPP.
Growth-plate defects and impaired growth follow childhood skeletal mineralization failure.
Abnormal vitamin B6 metabolism contributes to vitamin B6-dependent seizures.
Pediatric presentations can include delayed motor development alongside severe systemic disease.
PPi excess causes chondrocalcinosis.
Arthropathy and adult musculoskeletal disease contribute to generalized pain.

edges.target: ALPL loss of function and TNAP deficiency

edges.description: Persistently low serum ALP reports deficient TNAP enzymatic activity.
edges.source: ALPL loss of function and TNAP deficiency

edges.description: Elevated PPi reports impaired TNAP pyrophosphatase activity and substrate accumulation.
Elevated PLP reports impaired TNAP-mediated dephosphorylation of vitamin B6 substrate.
Elevated urine PEA reports accumulation of ALP substrates downstream of TNAP deficiency.

edges.target: ALPL loss of function and TNAP deficiency

evidence.reference_title: Hypophosphatasia: a genetic-based nosology and new insights in genotype-phenotype correlation.
evidence.snippet: Hypophosphatasia (HPP) is caused by pathogenic variants in the ALPL gene.
More than 400 ALPL mutations, mostly missense, are reported to date, transmitted by either autosomal dominant or recessive mode.

evidence.reference_title: Hypophosphatasia: a genetic-based nosology and new insights in genotype-phenotype correlation.
evidence.snippet: Homozygosity was an aggravating factor of the severity and moderate alleles were rare both in number and frequency.

nodes.description: Loss-of-function variants in ALPL reduce tissue-nonspecific alkaline phosphatase (TNAP) activity. Because TNAP functions as both an alkaline phosphatase and a pyrophosphatase at physiological pH, reduced activity lowers circulating alkaline phosphatase and prevents normal hydrolysis of mineralization and vitamin B6 substrates.
nodes.id: ALPL loss of function and TNAP deficiency

evidence.snippet: Hypophosphatasia (HPP) is a rare inherited metabolic disorder characterized by deficient activity of tissue-nonspecific alkaline phosphatase (TNAP) caused by variants in the ALPL gene.
Hypophosphatasia (HPP) results from ALPL mutations leading to deficient activity of the tissue-non-specific alkaline phosphatase isozyme (TNAP)

nodes.description: Bone-targeted enzyme replacement therapy that substitutes TNAP activity. Human interventional evidence shows improved survival, respiratory outcomes, skeletal mineralization, rickets, and functional outcomes in severely affected pediatric patients.

evidence.reference_title: Asfotase Alfa Treatment Improves Survival for Perinatal and Infantile Hypophosphatasia.
evidence.snippet: Asfotase alfa was associated with improved survival in treated patients vs historical controls: 95% vs 42% at age 1 year and 84% vs 27% at age 5 years, respectively (P < .0001, Kaplan-Meier log-rank test).

evidence.reference_title: Asfotase Alfa Treatment Improves Survival for Perinatal and Infantile Hypophosphatasia.
evidence.snippet: 76% (16/21) of the ventilated and treated patients survived, among whom 75% (12/16) were weaned from ventilatory support.

evidence.reference_title: Medical Management of Hypophosphatasia: Review of Data on Asfotase Alfa
evidence.snippet: Treatment for HPP relies on the enzyme replacement asfotase alfa.

nodes.description: PPi excess predisposes to chondrocalcinosis and arthropathy in HPP.

evidence.reference_title: Physiological role of alkaline phosphatase explored in hypophosphatasia.
evidence.snippet: PP(i) excesses cause chondrocalcinosis and sometimes arthropathy.

nodes.description: Premature cranial suture fusion occurs in severe infantile HPP despite generalized skeletal undermineralization.

evidence.snippet: Disease manifestations encompass skeletal hypomineralization with rickets and lung hypoplasia, vitamin B6-dependent seizures, craniosynostosis, and premature loss of deciduous teeth.
craniosynostosis and muscle weakness when HPP is severe

nodes.description: Chronic fatigue is a non-skeletal symptom that contributes to adult HPP disease burden.

evidence.reference_title: Medical Management of Hypophosphatasia: Review of Data on Asfotase Alfa
evidence.snippet: non-skeletal impairments, such as pain and chronic fatigue.

nodes.description: Recurrent or poorly healing fractures and adult pseudofractures result from defective mineralization and osteomalacia.

evidence.snippet: recurrent, poorly healing fractures, muscle weakness and arthropathy are common in adults.
In adults, pseudofractures are a characteristic indicator of severely compromised enzyme activity
elevated levels of ALP substrates, specifically inorganic pyrophosphate (PPi)

evidence.reference_title: Physiological role of alkaline phosphatase explored in hypophosphatasia.
evidence.snippet: Increased extracellular levels of pyridoxal 5'-phosphate and inorganic pyrophosphate (PP(i)) demonstrate that TNSALP is a phosphomonoester phosphohydrolase and a pyrophosphatase

readouts.interpretation: Elevated PPi reports impaired TNAP pyrophosphatase activity and substrate accumulation.

nodes.description: Persistently low serum alkaline phosphatase activity is the biochemical and phenotypic hallmark, present even in mild adult disease.

evidence.snippet: Persistently low serum alkaline phosphatase is the cardinal biochemical feature of the disease.

evidence.reference_title: Hypophosphatasia: a genetic-based nosology and new insights in genotype-phenotype correlation.
evidence.snippet: mild HPP, characterized by low alkaline phosphatase and unspecific clinical signs, is dominantly inherited and very common

nodes.description: Pediatric HPP can include delayed motor development, hypotonia, and gait disturbance as part of the systemic disease presentation.

evidence.snippet: The clinical presentation can comprise failure to thrive with muscular hypotonia, delayed motor development, and gait disturbances later in childhood.

nodes.description: Supportive management is multimodal and tailored to disease manifestations, including metabolic and musculoskeletal care, dental care, neurologic and neurosurgical surveillance, pain management, physical therapy, and psychological support.

evidence.snippet: The multidisciplinary team for comprehensive management of HPP patients should include expertise to ensure disease state metabolic and musculoskeletal treatment, dental care, neurological and neurosurgical surveillance, pain management, physical therapy, and psychological care.

nodes.description: Muscle weakness occurs in severe pediatric and adult HPP and contributes to functional limitation alongside skeletal disease.

evidence.snippet: craniosynostosis and muscle weakness when HPP is severe
recurrent, poorly healing fractures, muscle weakness and arthropathy are common in adults.

nodes.description: Generalized musculoskeletal pain is increasingly recognized in adult and pediatric-onset adult HPP, reflecting skeletal disease and arthropathy.

evidence.snippet: generalized musculoskeletal pain, weakness, and fatigue

nodes.description: Premature loss of primary teeth reflects defective dentoalveolar mineralization, especially cementum and supporting periodontal tissues, and can be the dominant manifestation in odontohypophosphatasia.

evidence.snippet: Disease manifestations encompass skeletal hypomineralization with rickets and lung hypoplasia, vitamin B6-dependent seizures, craniosynostosis, and premature loss of deciduous teeth.

evidence.reference_title: Dental manifestations of hypophosphatasia: translational and clinical advances
evidence.snippet: Premature tooth loss of fully rooted teeth is pathognomonic for HPP.
elevated levels of ALP substrates, specifically inorganic pyrophosphate (PPi), pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP)

evidence.reference_title: Physiological role of alkaline phosphatase explored in hypophosphatasia.
evidence.snippet: Increased extracellular levels of pyridoxal 5'-phosphate and inorganic pyrophosphate (PP(i)) demonstrate that TNSALP is a phosphomonoester phosphohydrolase and a pyrophosphatase

readouts.interpretation: Elevated PLP reports impaired TNAP-mediated dephosphorylation of vitamin B6 substrate.

nodes.description: Pyridoxine can be used for vitamin B6-dependent seizures in severe infantile HPP, bypassing impaired PLP handling downstream of TNAP deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Infantile hypophosphatasia secondary to a novel compound heterozygous mutation presenting with pyridoxine-responsive seizures.
evidence.snippet: Empiric treatment with favorable response to pyridoxine

nodes.description: PPi excess can drive chondrocalcinosis and arthropathy. This branch connects the TNAP substrate abnormality to adult musculoskeletal pain and joint manifestations distinct from primary osteomalacia.

evidence.reference_title: Physiological role of alkaline phosphatase explored in hypophosphatasia.
evidence.snippet: PP(i) excesses cause chondrocalcinosis and sometimes arthropathy.

nodes.description: Perinatal and infantile HPP can cause respiratory failure through lung hypoplasia and poorly mineralized ribs/chest wall, making respiratory complications a major mortality driver.

evidence.reference_title: Asfotase Alfa Treatment Improves Survival for Perinatal and Infantile Hypophosphatasia.
evidence.snippet: in its most severe perinatal and infantile forms, results in 50-100% mortality, typically from respiratory complications.
Disease manifestations encompass skeletal hypomineralization with rickets and lung hypoplasia

nodes.description: Defective bone mineralization presents as rickets in children and osteomalacia in adults. Severity generally tracks residual TNAP activity.

evidence.snippet: Thus, HPP features rickets or osteomalacia and hypomineralization of teeth.

evidence.reference_title: Physiological role of alkaline phosphatase explored in hypophosphatasia.
evidence.snippet: Hypophosphatasia (HPP) is the instructive rickets or osteomalacia caused by loss-of-function mutation(s) within TNSALP

nodes.description: Severe infantile HPP can include vitamin B6-dependent seizures from disrupted PLP handling and CNS vitamin B6 availability.

evidence.snippet: Disease manifestations encompass skeletal hypomineralization with rickets and lung hypoplasia, vitamin B6-dependent seizures, craniosynostosis, and premature loss of deciduous teeth.

evidence.reference_title: Infantile hypophosphatasia secondary to a novel compound heterozygous mutation presenting with pyridoxine-responsive seizures.
evidence.snippet: Defective TNSALP activity in the brain can result in intractable seizures responsive to pyridoxine.
Persistently low serum alkaline phosphatase is the cardinal biochemical feature of the disease.
The diagnosis is based on clinical manifestations in combination with persistently low alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity

readouts.interpretation: Persistently low serum ALP reports deficient TNAP enzymatic activity.
readouts.target: ALPL loss of function and TNAP deficiency

nodes.description: Childhood-onset HPP can impair growth through rickets and growth-plate defects, resulting in short stature or poor linear growth.

evidence.reference_title: Dental manifestations of hypophosphatasia: translational and clinical advances
evidence.snippet: HPP onset in childhood contributes to rickets, including growth plate defects and impaired growth.

nodes.description: Excess PPi inhibits hydroxyapatite crystal growth after matrix vesicle rupture, producing defective mineralization of bone, growth plates, teeth, and the dentoalveolar complex. This mechanism accounts for rickets or osteomalacia, impaired growth, fractures, premature tooth loss, and severe chest-wall disease.

evidence.reference_title: Physiological role of alkaline phosphatase explored in hypophosphatasia.
evidence.snippet: The skeletal disease is due to PP(i) inhibition of hydroxyapatite crystal growth extracellularly so that crystals form within matrix vesicles but fail to enlarge after these structures rupture.

evidence.reference_title: Dental manifestations of hypophosphatasia: translational and clinical advances
evidence.snippet: TNAP plays a crucial role in biomineralization of bones and teeth, in part by reducing levels of inorganic pyrophosphate (PPi), an inhibitor of biomineralization.

nodes.description: Deficient TNAP causes accumulation of natural substrates, especially inorganic pyrophosphate (PPi), pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP), and urinary phosphoethanolamine (PEA). PPi blocks apatite crystal growth, whereas PLP accumulation reflects disrupted vitamin B6 handling.

evidence.snippet: elevated levels of ALP substrates, specifically inorganic pyrophosphate (PPi), pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP) or urine phosphoethanolamine (PEA)
Reduced activity of TNAP leads to the accumulation of its substrates, mainly inorganic pyrophosphate and pyridoxal-5'-phosphate
elevated levels of ALP substrates, specifically inorganic pyrophosphate (PPi), pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP) or urine phosphoethanolamine (PEA)

readouts.interpretation: Elevated urine PEA reports accumulation of ALP substrates downstream of TNAP deficiency.

nodes.description: TNAP normally participates in vitamin B6 metabolism through extracellular PLP dephosphorylation. HPP disrupts this process, creating vitamin B6-dependent seizure susceptibility in severe infantile disease and contributing to delayed motor development in broader pediatric presentations.

evidence.reference_title: Physiological role of alkaline phosphatase explored in hypophosphatasia.
evidence.snippet: Aberrations of vitamin B(6) metabolism in HPP revealed that TNSALP is an ectoenzyme.
Disease manifestations encompass skeletal hypomineralization with rickets and lung hypoplasia, vitamin B6-dependent seizures, craniosynostosis, and premature loss of deciduous teeth.

evidence.reference_title: Infantile hypophosphatasia secondary to a novel compound heterozygous mutation presenting with pyridoxine-responsive seizures.
evidence.snippet: TNSALP is also necessary for proper vitamin B6 metabolism and its participation as a cofactor for neurotransmitters in the central nervous system.
